Optimal average power for Titanium Carbide surface cleaning without substrate damage
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Titanium Carbide absorption and minimal substrate interaction
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning of Titanium Carbide surfaces
Optimal repetition rate for continuous cleaning coverage with thermal management
Minimum fluence required for effective Titanium Carbide contaminant removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for efficient ablation of Titanium Carbide contaminants
Optimal scanning speed for efficient cleaning coverage
Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

Typical range for industrial cleaning of hard ceramics like TiC to balance ablation and avoid thermal damage; starting at 100 W for light contamination. Max limited by substrate melting point (~3067°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs for 1064 nm cleaning heads; academic review in 'Laser Cleaning of Metals' (Optics & Lasers in Engineering, 2018).

Effective speeds for contamination removal on reflective/high-conductivity materials like TiC; higher for light contamination (up to 5000 mm/s), lower for heavy (500-1000 mm/s). Optimal balances fluence and coverage. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals; case studies in Journal of Laser Applications (2020).

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of TiC with minimal HAZ due to its high thermal conductivity (~20-30 W/m·K); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond for ultra-precise but less common industrially. Avoids excessive heat accumulation. Source: Laser physics in 'Pulsed Laser Ablation of Ceramics' (Applied Surface Science, 2019).

Typical for 1064 nm cleaning to achieve uniform energy delivery on TiC; 50 kHz balances speed and heat management—higher rates risk accumulation on low-reflectivity TiC (~0.3-0.5 at 1064 nm). Trade-off: faster processing vs. potential overheating. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2017) on carbide ablation.

Fluence range for contaminant removal without damaging TiC; damage threshold ~4-5 J/cm² for ns pulses due to high melting point. Recommended conservative for safe cleaning of oxides or oils. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2021) for TiC at 1064 nm.

Beam diameter for precise surface prep on TiC; smaller (50-100 μm) for fine details, larger (200-500 μm) for broad areas. 200 μm optimal for uniform cleaning without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser systems for industrial cleaning.

Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-3) for heavy layers on TiC to ensure complete removal without substrate erosion. Recommended 2 passes for industrial prep. Source: Industry case studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (2019) for carbide tools.

Ensures uniform coverage on TiC surfaces; 60% typical for even fluence distribution and minimal striping, adjustable 30% for speed vs. 80% for thoroughness. Source: Process optimization in Laser & Photonics Reviews (2020).
